output:
**Applying reasoning injection before analysis:**

> **Amplify:** premise–inference mapping; formal validity check; fallacy detection
> **Suppress:** informal reasoning shortcuts; rhetorical validity conflation

---

## Decomposed Argument Structure

| # | Premise |
|---|---------|
| P1 | A full-time worker at $7.25/hr earns $15,080/year |
| P2 | $15,080 < poverty line for family of two ($20,440) |
| P3 | At $20/hr, same worker earns $41,600/year |
| P4 | $41,600 > poverty line for family of four ($31,200) |
| **C** | **A $20 minimum wage eliminates working poverty** |

The inference from P3 → C contains a **hidden premise that the argument never states or defends:**

## The Critical Unstated Assumption

**The argument assumes that workers will remain employed at the same full-time hours after the wage increase.**

The entire case rests on the arithmetic: **$20 × 40 hrs × 52 weeks = $41,600**. But this calculation only holds if employers continue offering the same jobs at the same hours at the higher wage. If employers respond by:

- **cutting hours** (moving workers to part-time),
- **eliminating positions**, or
- **automating roles**,

...then some workers don't earn $41,600 — they earn *less than before* or *nothing at all*. The argument treats the wage rate as the only variable while **silently holding employment quantity constant**. That's the load-bearing assumption the conclusion depends on, and it is never acknowledged, let alone justified.

Other assumptions exist (e.g., ignoring price-level effects on the poverty line), but this one is the most critical because it directly invalidates the mathematical foundation of the argument: you can't calculate annual income from hourly wage if the hours themselves change.
